APPENDIX C - Physical Properties of Flinak- 44
Long tubes $(\mathrm{x / d} > 100)$ of nickel, Inconel and 316 stainless steel were used.
Heat flux : 9,000 - 192,000 Btu/hr-ft²
Fused salts have potential application as heat transfer media wherever heat must be transferred to, or removed from, a system at temperatures above $600^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
First reported (5)* were the results of an experimental determination of heat transfer coefficients for molten sodium hydroxide.
It was concluded that, with respect to heat transfer, sodium hydroxide may be classified as an ordinary fluid $(0.5 < N_{\mathrm{Pr}} < 100)$ .
This report describes heat transfer with the eutectic mixture of sodium, potassium and lithium fluoride (11.5-42.0-46.5 mole %) known as Flinak.
Some results on Flinak heat transfer were reported in an earlier memorandum (6).
Flinak melts at approximately $850^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and is temperature stable to above $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
At $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ the viscosity of Flinak is about three times the viscosity of water at $60^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

The experimental system designed to measure the heat transfer coefficient of molten Flinak flowing in tubes was similar in all major respects to that previously described (5) for molten sodium hydroxide heat transfer studies.
Figures 1 and 2 present several views of the experimental system.
(3) The test section was connected to the system by Swage-lok unions.
The test section consisted of a 24 inch length of small diameter tubing which was heated by the passage of an electric current through the tube wall.
Power was supplied by a transformer rated at 4 Kva and was introduced to the test section through copper terminals silver-soldered to the tube at each end.
In the course of the experiment tubes which were made of nickel, Inconel, and 316 stainless steel were used as the test section.
With the Inconel and 316 s.s. test sections, flow at a Reynolds modulus of 10,000 could be attained.
As a result of the temperature limitation on the nickel test section, a maximum Reynolds modulus of only 6000 was possible.
In each case over $99\%$ of the heat generation occurred in the tube wall.

The system contained 350 pounds of Flinak, charged to the system as a well-mixed powder and melted while alternately evacuating the region above the melt and then purging and mixing the fluid with helium.
The results showed a discrepancy of approximately $1^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ between the observed and true readings at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Table III gives the result of this calibration check for 4 couples (different in each case) at the aluminum point.

It is seen that the output of the tube surface thermocouples remained essentially unchanged even after operation at temperatures up to $1450^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and despite a great deal of temperature cycling.
The technique employed has been described in Part I (5).

Thus, the assumption of uniform heat generation in the derivation of equation 2 is seen to be reasonable.

The data obtained (test series F) are shown in Figure 8.
In each case, the heat transfer coefficient was calculated for a position far down-stream $(\mathrm{x / d} > 100)$ where fully developed turbulent conditions existed.
An attempt to obtain data in the region of fully developed turbulent flow by raising the temperature above $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ was unsuccessful due to failure in fatigue of the nickel tube.

Therefore, a 316 stainless steel test section was installed and a series of runs made (Test J).

The first reported measurements for fused salt heat transfer were those of Kirst, Nagle and Castner (10) for the mixture, $\mathrm{NaNO}_2$ - $\mathrm{NaNO}_3$ - $\mathrm{KNO}_3$ (40-7-53 weight percent), known as "HTS." They employed a 6 foot length of

The results are shown in Figure 13 and can be correlated by the equation
Grele and Gideon (3) also obtained heat transfer data for sodium hydroxide flowing in an electrically heated tube.
They used an Inconel tube having an outside diameter of $\frac{3}{8}$ and a wall thickness of $\frac{1}{16}$ .

Recently, Salmon (14) using a double-tube heat exchanger, with sodium-potassium alloy (NaK) as coolant, measured heat transfer coefficients for the fluoride salt mixture NaF-ZrF $_4$ -UF $_4$ (50-46-4 mole percent) in a 0.269" I.D. nickel tube with a length to diameter ratio of 40.
The heat transfer coefficient was obtained by analysis of the data using the measured surface temperature and also by the graphical analysis technique suggested by Wilson (15).
The results, recalculated from Salmon's data as the Colburn j-function, are shown in Figure 15.
NaF-ZrF4-UF4(50-46-4 mole %) Heat Transfer.
Consider, the fuel to NaK heat exchanger designed for a 50 megawatt circulating fuel reactor.
In this heat exchanger the fuel is circulated on the outside of Inconel tubes (3/16" O.D. x 0.017" wall thickness) through which NaK is flowing.
The Reynolds modulus of the fuel is 5000 and it enters the heat exchanger at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and exits at $1100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
If the fuel is the mixture NaF-LiF-KF-UF $_4$ (11-45-41-3 mole %), then the fuel-side heat transfer coefficient is approximately 5000 Btu/hr-ft $^{2 - \circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
For the design conditions, the NaK (coolant-side) coefficient is 18,500 Btu/hr-ft $^{2 - \circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
R _ {T} = \frac {1}{h _ {F}} + \frac {1}{h _ {C}} + \left(\frac {\delta}{k}\right) _ {w} \tag {13}
Substituting into equation (13),
If a film forms on the fuel side of the Inconel tube, the thermal resistance of the system is increased by 0.00025 hr-ft²-⁰F/Btu.
This is equivalent to a 40.8% decrease in heat transfer.

Thus, if a film having a high thermal resistance forms in a heat exchanger designed to remove 50 megawatts of heat from the fuel, the heat removal is reduced to 29.6 megawatts and the outlet fluid temperature becomes 1263°F instead of 1100°F.
